executive_verdict: Ford supplier quality is a credible and painful wedge for KANIT, but the strongest pitch is not 'Ford will buy this immediately.' The strongest pitch is: Ford and Ford-linked suppliers operate inside a hard evidence regime where PPAP, APQP, FMEA, Control Plan, process capability, Q1/SIM scorecards, QIM/quality concerns, 8D responses, and IATF 16949 customer-specific requirements determine supplier status, audit pressure, launch readiness, and corrective-action credibility. Repeated weak evidence is a real buyer pain because it can trigger containment work, STA reviews, 100% inspection, Q1 score pressure, performance complaints, and potential IATF certificate consequences. KANIT should start as a supplier-side evidence readiness and 8D/CAPA quality-intelligence tool, then earn its way toward OEM or Tier-1 supplier-development workflows.
relevance_to_kanit: This area strengthens KANIT because the current product idea already maps naturally to the Ford quality workflow: analyze 8D/CAPA text and evidence, detect missing root-cause proof, weak containment, missing effectiveness verification, incomplete APQP/PPAP evidence, and convert recurring gaps into role/team learning tasks. The wedge is especially strong where suppliers manage evidence through email, spreadsheets, portals, and PDF packages while needing to satisfy Ford-specific evidence rules. The weakness is sales access: Ford itself may be hard to reach, procurement/security cycles may be long, and Ford supplier portals already exist. KANIT's more realistic first buyer is a Ford supplier, supplier-quality consultant, or Tier-1 supplier-development team that needs pre-submission quality checks before evidence reaches Ford [uncertain].
problem_evidence: The pain is real and externally forced. Ford's 2025 IATF customer-specific requirements define the 8D process, require IATF 16949 certification evidence in Ford systems for Q1, require suppliers to monitor Q1/SIM performance, and recommend at least weekly SIM status review. Ford requires notification within 24 hours when non-conforming product has shipped, 24-hour response to quality concerns, containment, certified stock, an 8D through D3, and within 15 calendar days an 8D or 6-panel with root cause, corrective/preventive action plan, and supporting data. Ford also requires ongoing process capability, APQP/PPAP evidence workbooks, traceability of special characteristics from DFMEA/PFMEA to Control Plan and process instructions, and process capability evidence. Ford's scorecard guide says Q1 score below 85 or zero-tolerance failures can lead to red supplier criteria and performance complaints to the IATF Oversight Office. Beyond Ford, AIAG says most automotive manufacturers and suppliers require one or more Core Tools, and its CQI-20 problem-solving guide explicitly targets repeat problems and skipped problem-solving steps.
ford_specific_evidence: Ford corporate evidence is strong. Ford's Supplier Quality portal exposes QIM, Q1 Requirements, SIM, 8D submission/search links, ePPAP, PPAP checklist, MSA, Ford IATF 16949 specifics, and Ford PPAP specifics. Ford's QIM application is used by Ford and FCSD suppliers to track and correct part quality concerns. Ford's June 2025 CSR states that Q1-related red/orange SIM metrics require work with STA, potentially including problem-solving investigation, Q1 Manufacturing Site Assessment submission, and reviews with STA. It also says Ford can submit an IATF performance complaint after Q1 revocation or a specific quality/delivery performance issue associated with the supplier's QMS. Ford Otosan evidence is directionally supportive but less specific to 8D/CAPA: its public Dealers and Suppliers page says it actively monitors supplier compliance, conducted supplier trainings/assessments/audits, reviewed supporting evidence documents for SAQ in 2025, and had 882 Tier-1 vehicle-parts suppliers in 2024. A hosted 2025 Ford Otosan sustainability-report snippet says Ford Otosan expects Tier-1 suppliers to comply with ISO 14001, IATF 16949, and Q1 requirements and delivered supplier training/visits, but because the accessible source was not on the fordotosan.com.tr domain, the exact 2025 report details should be treated as [uncertain].
buyer_personas: Likely buyer personas are supplier quality managers, quality directors, plant quality managers, APQP/PPAP engineers, launch quality engineers, STA-facing supplier representatives, and operations leaders at Ford suppliers. Day-to-day users are quality engineers preparing 8D/CAPA responses, PPAP coordinators, manufacturing/process engineers, metrology/lab staff, and team leaders responsible for containment and evidence collection. Economic buyers are plant managers, general managers, supplier-development directors, or owners of Turkish automotive supplier SMEs. Blockers include IT/security, legal, Ford portal owners, quality leaders who distrust AI-generated compliance guidance, and engineers who already use Excel/QMS workflows. Exact Ford Otosan buyer titles and buying authority are [uncertain].
willingness_to_pay_evidence: Willingness to pay is supported by adjacent paid categories, not yet by direct proof that buyers will pay for KANIT specifically [uncertain]. AIAG sells Core Tool and CQI-20 materials/training; CQI-20 corporate e-document subscriptions are listed at USD 1,700 for members and USD 2,800 for non-members, showing organizations pay to improve problem-solving capability. 1factory sells supplier quality, PPAP/APQP, FAI, SCAR, and 8D workflows and publishes customer proof such as PPAP time dropping from a week to one or two hours. Supplios markets an automotive supplier portal for 8D, PPAP, APQP, scorecards, certifications, and supplier follow-up, explicitly replacing Excel/email busywork. ETQ/Octave Reliance publishes QMS outcomes including 50% reduction in CAPA/SCAR resolution time and 32% reduction in scrap/rework in customer examples. Ford's own annual report shows warranty and field service action accrual of USD 17.19 billion at December 31, 2025, and states supplier recoveries are recognized when agreed and virtually certain, proving quality issues carry material financial stakes.
competitor_landscape: Direct and adjacent competitors include 1factory for supplier quality, PPAP/APQP, FAI, SCAR and 8D portals; Supplios for supplier portal, supplier quality, 8D/SCAR, PPAP/APQP, ISO/IATF certification tracking, scorecards, and follow-up automation; ETQ/Octave Reliance for enterprise QMS, CAPA, supplier quality, audit readiness, and IATF 16949-oriented compliance; MasterControl, Ideagen, AssurX, Plex, Siemens/Teamcenter quality, SAP quality modules, IQMS/DELMIAWorks, and enterprise QMS/PLM suites; Ford's own Supplier Portal, QIM, SIM, Global 8D, ePPAP, and Q1 systems; consultants and IATF/APQP/PPAP trainers; and internal Excel, SharePoint, email, and PDF package workflows. The landscape is crowded at the QMS/workflow layer, but I did not find a clear incumbent focused on converting repeated 8D/CAPA/PPAP evidence failures into skill gaps, micro-learning tasks, mentor approvals, and readiness scores for a specific supplier team [uncertain].
market_gap: The open wedge is 'pre-submission quality evidence intelligence' for suppliers: before a supplier submits an 8D, CAPA, PPAP package, QIM response, or audit evidence, KANIT checks whether required evidence is complete, traceable, current, and strong enough for Ford-style expectations. The second gap is pattern mining: repeated missing 5-Why proof, weak containment photos, poor PFMEA-to-Control-Plan traceability, missing process capability evidence, or late effectiveness verification should become a team/role readiness signal rather than just another closed nonconformance. The third gap is training feedback: AIAG and Ford emphasize competence, Core Tools, and Q1 performance, but most systems do not close the loop from a specific evidence defect to a targeted learning task. Evidence that no incumbent owns this exact incident-to-skill wedge is [uncertain].
product_implications: KANIT should add a Ford/IATF-inspired evidence checklist engine, deterministic rule packs for 8D/CAPA and PPAP evidence, APQP/PPAP Evidence Workbook field mapping, Control Plan/PFMEA/special-characteristic traceability checks, process capability evidence checks, missing evidence request templates, role/team skill-gap mapping, mentor approval workflow, audit trail, and a supplier-safe export report. The product should avoid claiming live Ford portal integration at first. It should support document upload, screenshot/photo review, structured extraction, source-cited findings, confidence levels, and human approval before any customer-facing response. It should also include mock Ford-style demo data only, because real Ford or supplier data would raise security and confidentiality issues [uncertain].
technical_depth_opportunities: Strong demo opportunities include a hybrid deterministic-plus-LLM quality agent, structured evidence extraction from PDFs/images/spreadsheets, cross-document traceability graph from problem statement to containment to root cause to corrective action to effectiveness evidence, APQP/PPAP evidence completeness scoring, automated detection of unsupported root-cause claims, computer-vision checks for weak or missing photo evidence, time-bound SLA checks for 24-hour/15-day response windows, repeated-failure pattern mining by role/team/station, and generation of learning tasks with mentor verification. A serious backend demo should show schema-first ingestion, rule versioning, explainable findings, source spans, immutable audit logs, tenant isolation, and human-in-the-loop approvals rather than a generic chatbot [uncertain].
claims_we_can_make: Supported claims for a demo or pitch: Ford supplier quality is evidence-heavy and portal-driven; Ford uses QIM, SIM, Q1, ePPAP, PPAP, Global 8D, and IATF customer-specific requirements; Ford's 2025 CSR requires fast quality concern response, containment, 8D outputs, supporting data, process capability evidence, and APQP/PPAP evidence; red/orange SIM metrics and Q1 score pressure can lead to STA action, Q1 revocation consequences, performance complaints, and IATF decertification workflow; Ford Otosan has a large Tier-1 supplier base and public supplier monitoring/development programs; automotive suppliers already pay for adjacent QMS, PPAP/APQP, supplier quality, training, and problem-solving tools.
claims_to_avoid: Avoid claiming that Ford or Ford Otosan has requested KANIT, that Ford will buy this, that KANIT integrates with QIM/SIM/Global 8D/ePPAP today, that KANIT is Ford-approved, that public evidence proves repeated evidence failures at Ford Otosan specifically, or that AI can certify IATF 16949/PPAP compliance without human review. Avoid saying all Ford suppliers have the same process, because FCSD, production, service, regional, Tier-1, sub-tier, and Ford Otosan workflows may differ. Avoid promising automatic acceptance of 8D/PPAP submissions or guaranteed Q1 score improvement. Avoid using real Ford supplier documents in demos unless explicitly authorized.
pivot_options: If Ford does not buy, KANIT can pivot to Ford suppliers, Ford Otosan suppliers, Turkish automotive Tier-1/Tier-2 suppliers, IATF 16949 consultants, APQP/PPAP training providers, QMS vendors needing an AI evidence layer, supplier-development teams at Tier-1s, or adjacent industrial customers with similar corrective-action evidence pressure. Nearby vertical pivots include aerospace supplier compliance, defense quality evidence, medical device CAPA, railway supplier quality, electronics manufacturing 8D/SCAR, and ESG/sustainability evidence review for supplier audits. The best pivot keeps the same core engine: incident evidence -> missing proof -> corrective action quality -> recurring pattern -> skill/readiness intervention [uncertain].
risks: Main risks: Ford portal and CSR workflows are complex, access-controlled, and may not be available to a startup; enterprise buyers may see the product as another QMS rather than a must-have; AI errors could create quality or legal liability; suppliers may resist uploading confidential Ford drawings, 8D reports, or customer concerns; incumbents can add AI evidence checks; initial users may prefer consultants or Excel; Ford Otosan-specific public evidence is limited; Turkish supplier budgets may be smaller than enterprise QMS budgets; and the product could become service-heavy if every customer has unique CSR formats. Risk reduction: start with pre-submission checks on synthetic or customer-owned documents, keep deterministic rules and human approval, sell paid readiness sprints, partner with consultants, and avoid official Ford integration claims until there is authorized access [uncertain].
